volumeTitle: Persians. Seven against Thebes. Suppliants. Prometheus Bound
label: Prometheus Bound (Section 136 - Section 330)

sections.label: Section 136
sections.original: αἰαῖ αἰαῖ , [*]
sections.translation: Alas ! Alas ! Offspring of fruitful Tethys and of him who with his sleepless current encircles the whole earth , children of your

sections.label: Section 140
sections.original: πατρὸς Ὠκεανοῦ ,
sections.translation: father Oceanus , behold , see with what fetters , upon the summit crag of this ravine , I am to hold my unenviable watch .

sections.label: Section 144
sections.original: φρουρὰν ἄζηλον ὀχήσω .
sections.translation: I see , Prometheus ;

sections.label: Section 1.145
sections.original: λεύσσω , Προμηθεῦ : φοβερὰ [*] δ ἐμοῖσιν ὄσσοις
sections.translation: and over my eyes a mist of tears and fear spread as I saw your body withering ignominiously upon this rock in these bonds of adamant . For there are new rulers in heaven , and Zeus governs with

sections.label: Section 1.150
sections.original: νεοχμοῖς δὲ δὴ νόμοις Ζεὺς ἀθέτως [*] κρατύνει ,
sections.translation: lawless customs ; that which was mighty before he now brings to nothing .

sections.label: Section 152
sections.original: εἰ γάρ μ ὑπὸ γῆν νέρθεν θ [*] Ἅιδου
sections.translation: Oh if only he had hurled me below the earth , yes beneath Hades , the entertainer of the dead , into impassable Tartarus ,

sections.label: Section 155
sections.original: δεσμοῖς ἀλύτοις ἀγρίως [*] πελάσας ,
sections.translation: and had ruthlessly fastened me in fetters no hand can loose , so that neither god nor any other might have gloated over this agony I feel ! But , now , a miserable plaything of the winds , I suffer pains to delight my enemies .

sections.label: Section 2.160
sections.original: τίς ὧδε τλησικάρδιος
sections.translation: Who of the gods is so hard of heart as to exult in this ? Who does not sympathize with your woes — save only Zeus ? But he in malice , has set his soul inflexibly

sections.label: Section 2.165
sections.original: δάμναται οὐρανίαν
sections.translation: and keeps in subjection the race sprung from Uranus ; nor will he stop , until he has satiated his soul or another seizes his impregnable empire by some device of guile .

sections.label: Section 168
sections.original: ἦ μὴν ἔτ ἐμοῦ , [*] καίπερ κρατεραῖς
sections.translation: Truly the day shall come when , although I am tortured in stubborn fetters ,

sections.label: Section 170
sections.original: χρείαν ἕξει μακάρων πρύτανις ,
sections.translation: the prince of the blessed will need me to reveal the new design whereby he shall be stripped of his sceptre and his dignities . Not by persuasion ’ s honeyed enchantments will he charm me ;

sections.label: Section 175
sections.original: οὔποτ ἀπειλὰς πτήξας τόδ ἐγὼ
sections.translation: and I will never , cowering before his dire threats , divulge this secret , until he releases me from my cruel bonds and provides compensation for this outrage .

sections.label: Section 2.180
sections.original: σὺ μὲν θρασύς τε καὶ πικραῖς
sections.translation: You are bold , and do not yield to your bitter pangs ; you give too much license to your tongue . But my soul is agitated by piercing fear , and I am in dread about your fate ,

sections.label: Section 2.185
sections.original: πᾷ [*] ποτε τῶνδε πόνων
sections.translation: wondering to what haven you must steer your ship to see an end of your voyage of sorrow . For the heart of Cronus ’ son is hardened against entreaty and his ways are inexorable .

sections.label: Section 189
sections.original: τὸ δίκαιον ἔχων Ζεύς : ἀλλ ἔμπας [ ὀίω ] [*]
sections.translation: I know that Zeus is harsh and

sections.label: Section 190
sections.original: μαλακογνώμων
sections.translation: keeps justice in his own hands ; but nevertheless one day his judgement will soften , when he has been crushed in the way that I know . [*] Then , calming down his stubborn wrath , he shall at last bond with me in union and friendship ,

sections.label: Section 195
sections.original: πάντ ἐκκάλυψον καὶ γέγων ἡμῖν λόγον ,
sections.translation: as eager as I am to welcome him .

sections.label: Section 196
sections.original: ποίῳ λαβών σε Ζεὺς ἐπ αἰτιάματι ,
sections.translation: Unfold the whole story and tell us upon what charge Zeus has caught you and painfully punishes you with such dishonor . Instruct us , unless , indeed , there is some harm in telling .

sections.label: Section 200
sections.original: ἄλγος δὲ σιγᾶν , πανταχῇ δὲ δύσποτμα .
sections.translation:
painful to keep it silent . My case is unfortunate every way .
When first the heavenly powers were moved to wrath , and mutual dissension was stirred up among them — some bent on casting Cronus from his seat so Zeus , in truth , might reign ; others , eager for

sections.label: Section 205
sections.original: σπεύδοντες , ὡς Ζεὺς μήποτ ἄρξειεν θεῶν ,
sections.translation: the contrary end , that Zeus might never win mastery over the gods — it was then that I , although advising them for the best , was unable to persuade the Titans , children of Heaven and Earth ; but they , disdaining counsels of craft , in the pride of their strength

sections.label: Section 210
sections.original: ᾤοντ ἀμοχθὶ πρὸς βίαν τε δεσπόσειν :
sections.translation: thought to gain the mastery without a struggle and by force . Often my mother Themis , or Earth ( though one form , she had many names ), had foretold to me the way in which the future was fated to come to pass . That it was not by brute strength nor through violence ,

sections.label: Section 215
sections.original: χρείη , [*] δόλῳ δὲ [*] τοὺς ὑπερσχόντας [*] κρατεῖν .
sections.translation: but by guile that those who should gain the upper hand were destined to prevail . And though I argued all this to them , they did not pay any attention to my words . With all that before me , it seemed best that , joining with my mother , I should place myself ,

sections.label: Section 220
sections.original: ἑκόνθ ἑκόντι Ζηνὶ συμπαραστατεῖν .
sections.translation: a welcome volunteer , on the side of Zeus ; and it is by reason of my counsel that the cavernous gloom of Tartarus now hides ancient Cronus and his allies within it . Thus I helped the tyrant of the gods

sections.label: Section 225
sections.original: κακαῖσι ποιναῖς ταῖσδέ μ ἀντημείψατο . [*]
sections.translation:
and with this foul payment he has responded ; for it is a disease that is somehow inherent in tyranny to have no faith in friends .
However , you ask why he torments me , and this I will now make clear .

sections.label: Section 230
sections.original: ὅπως τάχιστα τὸν πατρῷον ἐς θρόνον
sections.translation: As soon as he had seated himself upon his father ’ s throne , he immediately assigned to the deities their several privileges and apportioned to them their proper powers . But of wretched mortals he took no notice , desiring to bring

sections.label: Section 235
sections.original: τὸ πᾶν ἔχρῃζεν ἄλλο φιτῦσαι νέον .
sections.translation: the whole race to an end and create a new one in its place . Against this purpose none dared make stand except me — I only had the courage ; I saved mortals so that they did not descend , blasted utterly , to the house of Hades . This is why I am bent by such grievous tortures ,

sections.label: Section 240
sections.original: πάσχειν μὲν ἀλγειναῖσιν , οἰκτραῖσιν δ ἰδεῖν :
sections.translation: painful to suffer , piteous to behold . I who gave mortals first place in my pity , I am deemed unworthy to win this pity for myself , but am in this way mercilessly disciplined , a spectacle that shames the glory of Zeus .

sections.label: Section 244
sections.original: σιδηρόφρων τε κἀκ πέτρας εἰργασμένος
sections.translation: Iron-hearted and made of stone , Prometheus ,

sections.label: Section 245
sections.original: ὅστις , Προμηθεῦ , σοῖσιν οὐ συνασχαλᾷ
sections.translation: is he who feels no compassion at your miseries . For myself , I would not have desired to see them ; and now that I see them , I am pained in my heart .

sections.label: Section 248
sections.original: καὶ μὴν φίλοις γ [*] ἐλεινὸς [*] εἰσορᾶν ἐγώ .
sections.translation: Yes , to my friends indeed I am a spectacle of pity .

sections.label: Section 249
sections.original: μή πού τι προύβης τῶνδε καὶ περαιτέρω ;
sections.translation: Did you perhaps transgress even somewhat beyond this offence ?

sections.label: Section 250
sections.original: θνητοὺς [*] ἔπαυσα μὴ προδέρκεσθαι μόρον .
sections.translation: Yes , I caused mortals to cease foreseeing their doom . [*]

sections.label: Section 251
sections.original: τὸ ποῖον εὑρὼν τῆσδε φάρμακον νόσου ;
sections.translation: Of what sort was the cure that you found for this affliction ?

sections.label: Section 252
sections.original: τυφλὰς ἐν αὐτοῖς ἐλπίδας κατῴκισα .
sections.translation: I caused blind hopes to dwell within their breasts .

sections.label: Section 253
sections.original: μέγ ὠφέλημα τοῦτ ἐδωρήσω βροτοῖς .
sections.translation: A great benefit was this you gave to mortals .

sections.label: Section 254
sections.original: πρὸς τοῖσδε μέντοι πῦρ ἐγώ σφιν ὤπασα .
sections.translation: In addition , I gave them fire .

sections.label: Section 255
sections.original: καὶ νῦν φλογωπὸν πῦρ [*] ἔχουσ ἐφήμεροι ;
sections.translation: What ! Do creatures of a day now have flame-eyed fire ?

sections.label: Section 256
sections.original: ἀφ οὗ γε πολλὰς ἐκμαθήσονται τέχνας .
sections.translation: Yes , and from it they shall learn many arts .

sections.label: Section 257
sections.original: τοιοῖσδε δή σε Ζεὺς ἐπ αἰτιάμασιν
sections.translation: Then it was on a charge like this that Zeus —

sections.label: Section 258
sections.original: αἰκίζεταί τε κοὐδαμῇ χαλᾷ κακῶν .
sections.translation: Torments me and in no way gives me respite from pain .

sections.label: Section 259
sections.original: οὐδ ἔστιν ἄθλου τέρμα σοι προκείμενον ; [*]
sections.translation: And is there no end assigned to your ordeal ?

sections.label: Section 260
sections.original: οὐκ ἄλλο γ οὐδέν , πλὴν ὅταν κείνῳ δοκῇ .
sections.translation: No , none except when it seems good to him .

sections.label: Section 261
sections.original: δόξει δὲ πῶς ; τίς ἐλπίς ; οὐχ ὁρᾷς ὅτι
sections.translation: But how will it seem good to him ? What hope is there ? Do you not see that you have wronged ? And yet it is not pleasant for me to talk about how you have wronged , and for you it is pain . So , let us quit this theme ; and may you seek some release from your ordeal .

sections.label: Section 265
sections.original: ἐλαφρὸν ὅστις πημάτων ἔξω πόδα
sections.translation: It is easy for him who keeps his foot free from harm to counsel and admonish him who is in misery . I have known this all the while . Of my own will , yes , of my own will I erred — I will not deny it . By helping mortals I found suffering for myself ;

sections.label: Section 270
sections.original: οὐ μήν τι ποιναῖς γ ᾠόμην τοίαισί με [*]
sections.translation: nevertheless I did not think I would be punished in this way — wasting away upon cliffs in mid-air , my portion this desolate and dreary crag . And now , bewail no more my present woes ; alight on the ground and listen to my

sections.label: Section 275
sections.original: ἀκούσαθ , ὡς μάθητε διὰ τέλους τὸ πᾶν .
sections.translation: oncoming fortunes so that you may be told them from end to end . Consent , I beg you , oh consent . Take part in the trouble of him who is now in sore distress . In truth , affliction wanders impartially abroad and alights upon all in turn .

sections.label: Section 279
sections.original: οὐκ ἀκούσαις ἐπεθώυξας
sections.translation: Not to unwilling ears have you made this appeal ,

sections.label: Section 280
sections.original: τοῦτο , Προμηθεῦ .
sections.translation: Prometheus . And so now with light foot I will quit my swift-speeding seat and the pure air , the pathway of birds and draw near to this rugged ground ; for I want to hear

sections.label: Section 285
sections.original: χρῄζω διὰ παντὸς ἀκοῦσαι .
sections.translation: the whole story of your sorrows .

sections.label: Section 286
sections.original: ἥκω δολιχῆς τέρμα κελεύθου
sections.translation: I have come to the end of a long journey in my passage to you , Prometheus , guiding by my own will , without a bridle , this swift-winged bird .

sections.label: Section 290
sections.original: ταῖς σαῖς δὲ τύχαις , ἴσθι , συναλγῶ .
sections.translation: For your fate , you may be sure , I feel compassion . Kinship , I think , constrains me to this ; and , apart from blood ties , there is none to whom I should pay greater respect than to you .

sections.label: Section 295
sections.original: γνώσει δὲ τάδ ὡς ἔτυμ , οὐδὲ μάτην
sections.translation: You shall know this for simple truth and that it is not in me to utter vain and empty words ; come , tell me ; what aid can I render you ? For you shall never say that you have a friend more loyal than Oceanus .

sections.label: Section 300
sections.original: ἔα : τί χρῆμα λεύσσω ; καὶ σὺ δὴ πόνων ἐμῶν
sections.translation: Ha ! What have we here ? So then you too have come to stare upon my sufferings ? How did you summon courage to quit the stream that bears your name and the rock-roofed caves you yourself have made and come to this land , the mother of iron ? Is it that you have come

sections.label: Section 305
sections.original: ἐμὰς ἀφῖξαι καὶ συνασχαλῶν κακοῖς ;
sections.translation: to gaze upon my state and join your grief to my distress ? Look upon me here — a spectacle , the friend of Zeus , who helped him to establish his sovereign power , by what anguish I am bent by him !

sections.label: Section 309
sections.original: ὁρῶ , Προμηθεῦ , καὶ παραινέσαι γέ σοι
sections.translation: I see , Prometheus ; and I want to give you

sections.label: Section 310
sections.original: θέλω τὰ λῷστα , καίπερ ὄντι ποικίλῳ .
sections.translation: the best advice , although you yourself are wily . Learn to know yourself and adapt yourself to new ways ; for new also is the ruler among the gods . If you hurl forth words so harsh and of such whetted edge , perhaps Zeus may hear you ,

sections.label: Section 315
sections.original: θακῶν κλύοι Ζεύς , ὥστε σοι τὸν νῦν ὄχλον [*]
sections.translation: though throned far off , high in the heavens , and then your present multitude of sorrows shall seem but childish sport . Oh wretched sufferer ! Put away your wrathful mood and try to find release from these miseries . Perhaps this advice may seem to you old and dull ;

sections.label: Section 320
sections.original: τοιαῦτα μέντοι τῆς ἄγαν ὑψηγόρου
sections.translation: but your plight , Prometheus , is only the wages of too boastful speech . You still have not learned humility , nor do you bend before misfortune , but would rather add even more miseries to those you have . Therefore take me as your teacher

sections.label: Section 325
sections.original: πρὸς κέντρα κῶλον ἐκτενεῖς , ὁρῶν ὅτι
sections.translation: and do not add insult to injury , seeing that a harsh monarch now rules who is accountable to no one . So now I will depart and see whether I can release you from these sufferings . And may you hold your peace and be not too blustering of speech .

sections.label: Section 330
sections.original: ἢ οὐκ οἶσθ ἀκριβῶς ὢν περισσόφρων ὅτι
sections.translation: Or , can it be that for all your exceeding wisdom , you do not know that chastisement is inflicted on a wagging tongue ?
